title: Peptic Ulcer Disease
oneLiner: Mucosal breaks in stomach/duodenum from acid-peptic injury, often due to H. pylori or NSAIDs; can bleed, perforate, or obstruct.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old man presents with burning epigastric pain that wakes him at 2 AM and is relieved by eating a cracker. He takes ibuprofen daily for back pain and has had black, tarry stools for two days. Heart rate is 105 bpm, BP 100/60, and abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness without rigidity.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Burning epigastric pain** → classic ulcer pain (visceral afferent from duodenal/gastric mucosa)
- **Nocturnal awakening** → duodenal ulcer pattern (acid secretion peaks overnight, empty stomach)
- **Relieved by food** → duodenal ulcer (food buffers acid; gastric ulcers often worsen with food)
- **Daily ibuprofen** → NSAID-induced ulcer (COX-1 inhibition reduces protective prostaglandins)
- **Black, tarry stools** → melena from upper GI bleeding (ulcer eroding into vessel, e.g., gastroduodenal artery)
- **Tachycardia ± hypotension** → signs of hypovolaemia (≥500 mL blood loss)
- **Mild epigastric tenderness** → no perforation yet (if perforated, would have board-like rigidity and peritonitis)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent bleeding in elderly:** No pain; presents with syncope, anaemia, or fatigue from chronic blood loss. Often underlying NSAID use.
2. **Gastric outlet obstruction:** Epigastric fullness, nausea/vomiting of undigested food, weight loss. Ulcer near pylorus/duodenal bulb causes scarring.
3. **Penetration into pancreas:** Pain shifts to back, becomes constant, radiates to back. Resembles pancreatitis but amylase normal.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is a defect in the gastric or duodenal mucosa that extends through the muscularis mucosae, caused by an imbalance between aggressive factors (acid, pepsin, H. pylori) and protective factors (mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins).

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Lifetime prevalence ~5–10% worldwide; H. pylori accounts for 70–90% of duodenal ulcers and ~70% of gastric ulcers.
- **Stakes:** Ulcers can silently bleed (30% of cases present as haematemesis/melena), perforate (mortality 10–40% if missed), or cause gastric outlet obstruction.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking epigastric pain for functional dyspepsia; failing to test for H. pylori and treat it; continuing NSAIDs after diagnosis; not considering Zollinger-Ellison syndrome in refractory cases.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**
- Normal mucosa resists damage by secreting a bicarbonate-rich mucus layer and maintaining tight junctions. Prostaglandins (especially PGE₂) stimulate mucus and bicarbonate secretion, inhibit acid secretion, and promote blood flow.
- **H. pylori** colonises the mucus layer of the antrum, uses urease to produce ammonia (neutralises acid), and releases cytotoxins (CagA, VacA) that disrupt epithelial cells, cause inflammation, and increase gastrin release → more acid → duodenal metaplasia → ulcer in duodenal bulb.
- **NSAIDs** inhibit COX-1 (constitutive) and COX-2 (inducible). COX-1 inhibition depletes prostaglandins → reduced mucus, bicarbonate, and blood flow → mucosa vulnerable to acid.
- **Stress ulcers** (e.g., in ICU) arise from ischaemia-reperfusion, splanchnic hypoperfusion, and mucosal barrier breakdown.
- **Zollinger-Ellison syndrome** (gastrinoma) → massive acid hypersecretion → ulcers in atypical locations (post-bulbar, jejunum).

**Key clinical signs and their mechanisms:**
- **Epigastric pain:** Acid directly irritates exposed nerve endings in ulcer base; pain worsens when acid concentration is high (empty stomach) and improves when food buffers acid (duodenal) or worsens due to distension and acid contact (gastric).
- **Melena:** Blood from ulcer (often posterior wall duodenal ulcer eroding into gastroduodenal artery) is digested by intestinal enzymes → black, tarry stool.
- **Hematemesis:** Coffee-ground vomitus (acid denatures haemoglobin) or fresh red blood (rapid bleeding).
- **Perforation:** Ulcer erodes through full thickness → free air under diaphragm → severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness.
- **Obstruction:** Chronic scarring near pylorus → delayed gastric emptying → early satiety, vomiting, weight loss.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
- Nocturnal pain = empty stomach + peak nocturnal acid.
- Relief with food = duodenal (acid buffered).
- Black stools = upper GI bleed (must exclude other sources like oesophageal varices or erosive gastritis).
- Back pain = possible posterior penetration.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | H. pylori (most common overall; 70–90% duodenal, 70% gastric) | Positive urea breath test or stool antigen; often young/middle-aged |
| **Drug-induced** | NSAIDs (including aspirin, COX-2 inhibitors at high doses) | “Takes ibuprofen for arthritis” or “recently started aspirin for stroke prevention” |
| **Stress** | Critical illness (ICU, burns, trauma, sepsis) | Patient on mechanical ventilation, coagulopathy, or severe burns (Curling’s ulcer) |
| **Hypersecretory** | Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) | Refractory, multiple ulcers, atypical location (post-bulbar, jejunum), diarrhoea, hypercalcaemia (MEN1) |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable cause (~5–10%) | Workup negative for H. pylori, NSAIDs, gastrinoma |
| **Less common** | Crohn’s disease, CMV (immunocompromised), radiotherapy, cocaine | Known IBD, post-transplant, or radiation field |
| **Lifestyle** | Smoking, heavy alcohol | Not primary causes but impair healing and increase recurrence (especially smoking) |
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Endoscopy is gold standard. An ulcer is defined as a mucosal defect ≥5 mm in diameter with visible depth. Scoring systems like Rockall (for bleeding risk) combine age, shock, comorbidities, and endoscopic stigmata.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Upper GI endoscopy (EGD)** | Number, size, location, shape of ulcer; signs of recent haemorrhage (visible vessel, adherent clot) or perforation | Definitively diagnose PUD; biopsy for H. pylori and rule out malignancy (gastric ulcers must be biopsied) | Duodenal ulcers are virtually never malignant; gastric ulcers require 6–8 biopsies from edges. Active bleeding? Visible vessel? → high-risk stigmata |
| **H. pylori testing (before antibiotics)** | Urease-based (CLO test) on biopsy; urea breath test; stool antigen | Must treat H. pylori to heal ulcer and prevent recurrence | Positive: treat with triple/quadruple therapy. Negative: consider NSAIDs, Zollinger-Ellison, or idiopathic |
| **FBC** | Hb, MCV, platelets | Assess anaemia (acute or chronic blood loss) | Low Hb ≤10 g/dL suggests significant bleeding; MCV low = iron deficiency from chronic loss |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT, APTT | Check for coagulopathy (anticoagulants, liver disease) | Elevated INR increases bleeding risk |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | In bleeding: signs of shock or poor perfusion | Lactate ≥2 mmol/L = early warning; ≥4 = high risk |

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms PUD:** Endoscopic visualisation of a mucosal defect with depth.
- **Single finding that rules out PUD:** Normal endoscopy (if done off PPI for ≥2 weeks, false negatives rare).
- **Caveat:** If urgent endoscopy cannot be done, a positive H. pylori stool antigen + typical history is reasonable to start empiric therapy, but endoscopy is mandatory for first gastric ulcer (to exclude cancer).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):** For active bleeding (melena/haematemesis, haemodynamic compromise).
- **Resuscitate:** IV access ×2 wide-bore, cross-match 4 units, start crystalloid (e.g., 500 mL bolus if hypotensive). Target Hb >70 g/L (or >90 if significant comorbidities).
- **PPI bolus:** Pantoprazole 80 mg IV stat, then 8 mg/h infusion for 72 hours. Reduces rebleeding risk by suppressing acid and stabilising clot.
- **Call GI/Gastroenterology:** Urgent endoscopy within 24 hours for high-risk patients (Rockall ≥4). Use Glasgow-Blatchford score to determine urgency.

**Do NOT:** Give prokinetics (metoclopramide) – delays endoscopy. Avoid nasogastric tube unless needed for lavage (controversial, may dislodge clot).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Continue PPI infusion** (esomeprazole 8 mg/h) for 72 hours after successful endoscopic haemostasis (clip, thermal, or injection).
- **H. pylori treatment:** If positive, start triple therapy (e.g., Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID + Amoxicillin 1 g PO BID + Clarithromycin 500 mg PO BID × 14 days). For penicillin allergy: metronidazole 400 mg TID + clarithromycin + PPI. Quadruple therapy (bismuth-based) for high resistance areas.
- **Discontinue NSAIDs/aspirin:** If aspirin for secondary prevention, discuss risk–benefit with cardiology; often restart after haemostasis.
- **Monitor for rebleeding:** Tachycardia, drop in Hb, fresh haematemesis, melena. Repeat endoscopy if signs persist.
- **Diet:** Clear liquids after endoscopy; no restriction unless obstructed.

**Long-term:**
- **Healed ulcers:** Continue PPI (e.g., Omeprazole 20 mg once daily) for 4–8 weeks for duodenal, 8–12 weeks for gastric. Confirm healing with repeat endoscopy for gastric ulcers (biopsy scar).
- **H. pylori eradication confirmed** with urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after finishing antibiotics (off PPI for 2 weeks to avoid false negative).
- **If NSAID-related:** Switch to COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) + PPI if NSAID must continue. Consider misoprostol (200 µg QID) but side effects (diarrhoea, cramps) limit use.
- **Refractory ulcers:** Consider Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL; if borderline, test with secretin stimulation). Treat with high-dose PPI ± surgical excision of gastrinoma.
- **Prophylaxis in ICU/at-risk:** PPI or H₂RA (e.g., pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily) for stress ulcer prophylaxis in ventilated, coagulopathic, or post-surgical patients.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- Not biopsying a gastric ulcer (5–10% are malignant).
- Stopping antibiotics too early (must complete 14-day course).
- Resuming NSAIDs without gastroprotection.
- Ignoring positive H. pylori test result (must treat).
- Not checking for H. pylori iatrogenic false-negative (if patient on PPIs or recent antibiotics).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Peptic Ulcer Disease | Gastro-oesophageal Reflux (GERD) | Acute Pancreatitis | Biliary Colic / Cholecystitis |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Epigastric, burning, nocturnal, relieved by food (duodenal) or worsened (gastric) | Retrosternal burning, worsens when lying down, after meals | Sudden severe epigastric pain, radiates to back, relieved by leaning forward | RUQ/epigastric pain, crescendo, after fatty meal, radiates to right shoulder/scapula |
| **Exam** | Mild epigastric tenderness; if perforation → board-like rigidity, rebound | Normal or mild epigastric tenderness | Epigastric tenderness, guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen sign (rare) | Murphy’s sign (inspiratory arrest on palpation), RUQ tenderness |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless co-existing cardiac) | Normal | May have ST depression from ischaemia (rare) | Normal |
| **Labs** | Anaemia (if bleeding), positive H. pylori test | Normal | Elevated amylase/lipase (≥3× ULN), LFTs may be raised | Elevated ALP, GGT, bilirubin (if obstruction); normal amylase |
| **Key diff** | Endoscopy shows ulcer | Endoscopy shows oesophagitis or normal; 24h pH monitoring if uncertain | Imaging (CT) shows inflamed pancreas, necrosis | RUQ ultrasound shows gallstones, thick-walled gallbladder |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Upper GI bleeding** | Haematemesis (coffee-ground or red), melena, haematochezia (if massive), symptomatic anaemia, shock | Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, postural drop; Hb drop | Resuscitate, IV PPI, urgent endoscopy; Rockall score ≥4 → high risk; consider interventional radiology or surgery if endoscopy fails |
| **Perforation** | Sudden severe epigastric pain, generalised peritonitis, board-like rigidity | Erect CXR shows free air under diaphragm (75% sensitivity); CT abdomen with oral contrast is gold standard | Emergency laparotomy (open or laparoscopic) with omental patch (Graham patch) for duodenal repair; IV antibiotics, fluids, ICU |
| **Gastric outlet obstruction** | Early satiety, nausea/vomiting, weight loss, epigastric distension | Successive vomiting, visible peristalsis, succussion splash | NG decompression, correct electrolytes (hypokalaemic hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis); endoscopy to balloon dilate or stent; if refractory, surgical bypass (gastrojejunostomy) |
| **Penetration** | Pain shifts to back, becomes constant, no peritonitis | Back tenderness, raised amylase (mild due to inflammation of pancreas) | CT abdomen; high-dose PPI, surgery rarely needed unless fistula develops |
| **Malignant transformation** | Gastric ulcer that does not heal after 8 weeks of PPI; weight loss, anorexia | Endoscopic biopsy shows adenocarcinoma | Staging CT, surgical resection, oncology referral |

**Late-presentation stems:** “50-year-old with epigastric pain for 3 months, now vomiting undigested food, dehydrated, hypochloraemic alkalosis” = gastric outlet obstruction. “Patient with known duodenal ulcer presents with sudden severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, no bowel sounds” = perforation.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **H. pylori causes:** NSAIDs, Stress, H. pylori, Acid (hypersecretion), Alcohol, Smoking, Zollinger-Ellison → **“NSHAAZ”** (pronounced “n-shahz”)
- **Gastric vs Duodenal Ulcer:**  
  *Gastric: “G = Grumbling pain worse after Gulp”* (pain 30 min–1 hour after meals)  
  *Duodenal: “D = Delayed, Dusk, Dull”* (pain 2–3 hours after meals, nocturnal, relieved by food)
- **Complications of PUD:** Bleeding, Perforation, Obstruction, Penetration → **“BPOP”** (like “pop” the ulcer)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **No pain does not mean no ulcer** – especially in elderly on NSAIDs; look for insidious anaemia.
2. **Gastric ulcers must be biopsied (6–8 quadrants); duodenal ulcers need not be** – unless atypical.
3. **H. pylori eradication cures >90% of duodenal ulcers** – recurrence <10% if treatment is successful.
4. **PPI therapy should continue for 72 hours after endoscopic haemostasis** – decreases rebleeding from 15% to 5%.
5. **If a gastric ulcer does not heal after 8 weeks of high-dose PPI, re-endoscope and biopsy** – rule out malignancy.
6. **Stress ulcer prophylaxis is indicated only in ICU patients with coagulopathy, mechanical ventilation >48h, or history of PUD** – not for all hospitalised patients.
7. **Bleeding ulcer with a visible vessel = high risk** (rebleeding >50% without endoscopic therapy).

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking epigastric pain for cardiac ischaemia – always get an ECG.
- Starting H. pylori treatment before confirming the patient is not on PPIs or antibiotics (causes false negatives).
- Forgetting to check for iron deficiency anaemia in chronic melena – ferritin may be low.
- Not asking about NSAID/aspirin use – patients often don’t volunteer it.
- Assuming all epigastric pain is PUD – think pancreatitis, cholecystitis, cardiac.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **Haemodynamic instability:** Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1 L crystalloid, or heart rate >120 bpm (pulse >120 is ≥1000 mL blood loss).
- **Active red haematemesis** (massive bleed) or recurrent haematemesis after initial resuscitation.
- **Perforation:** Any sign of peritonitis (rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds) – call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Gastric outlet obstruction with severe electrolyte disturbance:** Potassium <2.5 mmol/L, pH >7.5 (metabolic alkalosis), or cardiac arrhythmia.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single parameter scoring 3, or aggregate 5+).

**Specific thresholds for critical care involvement:**
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (not correcting after initial fluid challenge).
- **Hb drop to <70 g/L** (or <80 g/L if coronary disease) despite blood transfusion – ongoing bleeding.
- **GCS ≤13** or any new confusion (hypovolaemic shock affecting cerebral perfusion).
- **Need for high-dependency unit:** planned endoscopic intervention with high-risk stigmata (e.g., adherent clot, visible vessel) – likely requires ICU/HDU post-procedure.

**When to call the crash team:** Cardiac arrest from massive haemorrhage (unlikely but possible) – activate local cardiac arrest protocol (CPR, massive transfusion, reverse anticoagulation).

## Peptic Ulcer Disease
**One-liner:** Mucosal breaks in stomach/duodenum from acid-peptic injury, often due to H. pylori or NSAIDs; can bleed, perforate, or obstruct.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old man presents with burning epigastric pain that wakes him at 2 AM and is relieved by eating a cracker. He takes ibuprofen daily for back pain and has had black, tarry stools for two days. Heart rate is 105 bpm, BP 100/60, and abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness without rigidity.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Burning epigastric pain** → classic ulcer pain (visceral afferent from duodenal/gastric mucosa)
- **Nocturnal awakening** → duodenal ulcer pattern (acid secretion peaks overnight, empty stomach)
- **Relieved by food** → duodenal ulcer (food buffers acid; gastric ulcers often worsen with food)
- **Daily ibuprofen** → NSAID-induced ulcer (COX-1 inhibition reduces protective prostaglandins)
- **Black, tarry stools** → melena from upper GI bleeding (ulcer eroding into vessel, e.g., gastroduodenal artery)
- **Tachycardia ± hypotension** → signs of hypovolaemia (≥500 mL blood loss)
- **Mild epigastric tenderness** → no perforation yet (if perforated, would have board-like rigidity and peritonitis)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent bleeding in elderly:** No pain; presents with syncope, anaemia, or fatigue from chronic blood loss. Often underlying NSAID use.
2. **Gastric outlet obstruction:** Epigastric fullness, nausea/vomiting of undigested food, weight loss. Ulcer near pylorus/duodenal bulb causes scarring.
3. **Penetration into pancreas:** Pain shifts to back, becomes constant, radiates to back. Resembles pancreatitis but amylase normal.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is a defect in the gastric or duodenal mucosa that extends through the muscularis mucosae, caused by an imbalance between aggressive factors (acid, pepsin, H. pylori) and protective factors (mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins).

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Lifetime prevalence ~5–10% worldwide; H. pylori accounts for 70–90% of duodenal ulcers and ~70% of gastric ulcers.
- **Stakes:** Ulcers can silently bleed (30% of cases present as haematemesis/melena), perforate (mortality 10–40% if missed), or cause gastric outlet obstruction.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking epigastric pain for functional dyspepsia; failing to test for H. pylori and treat it; continuing NSAIDs after diagnosis; not considering Zollinger-Ellison syndrome in refractory cases.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story of development:**
- Normal mucosa resists damage by secreting a bicarbonate-rich mucus layer and maintaining tight junctions. Prostaglandins (especially PGE₂) stimulate mucus and bicarbonate secretion, inhibit acid secretion, and promote blood flow.
- **H. pylori** colonises the mucus layer of the antrum, uses urease to produce ammonia (neutralises acid), and releases cytotoxins (CagA, VacA) that disrupt epithelial cells, cause inflammation, and increase gastrin release → more acid → duodenal metaplasia → ulcer in duodenal bulb.
- **NSAIDs** inhibit COX-1 (constitutive) and COX-2 (inducible). COX-1 inhibition depletes prostaglandins → reduced mucus, bicarbonate, and blood flow → mucosa vulnerable to acid.
- **Stress ulcers** (e.g., in ICU) arise from ischaemia-reperfusion, splanchnic hypoperfusion, and mucosal barrier breakdown.
- **Zollinger-Ellison syndrome** (gastrinoma) → massive acid hypersecretion → ulcers in atypical locations (post-bulbar, jejunum).

**Key clinical signs and their mechanisms:**
- **Epigastric pain:** Acid directly irritates exposed nerve endings in ulcer base; pain worsens when acid concentration is high (empty stomach) and improves when food buffers acid (duodenal) or worsens due to distension and acid contact (gastric).
- **Melena:** Blood from ulcer (often posterior wall duodenal ulcer eroding into gastroduodenal artery) is digested by intestinal enzymes → black, tarry stool.
- **Hematemesis:** Coffee-ground vomitus (acid denatures haemoglobin) or fresh red blood (rapid bleeding).
- **Perforation:** Ulcer erodes through full thickness → free air under diaphragm → severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness.
- **Obstruction:** Chronic scarring near pylorus → delayed gastric emptying → early satiety, vomiting, weight loss.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
- Nocturnal pain = empty stomach + peak nocturnal acid.
- Relief with food = duodenal (acid buffered).
- Black stools = upper GI bleed (must exclude other sources like oesophageal varices or erosive gastritis).
- Back pain = possible posterior penetration.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | H. pylori (most common overall; 70–90% duodenal, 70% gastric) | Positive urea breath test or stool antigen; often young/middle-aged |
| **Drug-induced** | NSAIDs (including aspirin, COX-2 inhibitors at high doses) | “Takes ibuprofen for arthritis” or “recently started aspirin for stroke prevention” |
| **Stress** | Critical illness (ICU, burns, trauma, sepsis) | Patient on mechanical ventilation, coagulopathy, or severe burns (Curling’s ulcer) |
| **Hypersecretory** | Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) | Refractory, multiple ulcers, atypical location (post-bulbar, jejunum), diarrhoea, hypercalcaemia (MEN1) |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable cause (~5–10%) | Workup negative for H. pylori, NSAIDs, gastrinoma |
| **Less common** | Crohn’s disease, CMV (immunocompromised), radiotherapy, cocaine | Known IBD, post-transplant, or radiation field |
| **Lifestyle** | Smoking, heavy alcohol | Not primary causes but impair healing and increase recurrence (especially smoking) |

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:** Endoscopy is gold standard. An ulcer is defined as a mucosal defect ≥5 mm in diameter with visible depth. Scoring systems like Rockall (for bleeding risk) combine age, shock, comorbidities, and endoscopic stigmata.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Upper GI endoscopy (EGD)** | Number, size, location, shape of ulcer; signs of recent haemorrhage (visible vessel, adherent clot) or perforation | Definitively diagnose PUD; biopsy for H. pylori and rule out malignancy (gastric ulcers must be biopsied) | Duodenal ulcers are virtually never malignant; gastric ulcers require 6–8 biopsies from edges. Active bleeding? Visible vessel? → high-risk stigmata |
| **H. pylori testing (before antibiotics)** | Urease-based (CLO test) on biopsy; urea breath test; stool antigen | Must treat H. pylori to heal ulcer and prevent recurrence | Positive: treat with triple/quadruple therapy. Negative: consider NSAIDs, Zollinger-Ellison, or idiopathic |

| **Coagulation screen** | PT, APTT | Check for coagulopathy (anticoagulants, liver disease) | Elevated INR increases bleeding risk |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | In bleeding: signs of shock or poor perfusion | Lactate ≥2 mmol/L = early warning; ≥4 = high risk |

- **Single finding that confirms PUD:** Endoscopic visualisation of a mucosal defect with depth.
- **Single finding that rules out PUD:** Normal endoscopy (if done off PPI for ≥2 weeks, false negatives rare).
- **Caveat:** If urgent endoscopy cannot be done, a positive H. pylori stool antigen + typical history is reasonable to start empiric therapy, but endoscopy is mandatory for first gastric ulcer (to exclude cancer).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):** For active bleeding (melena/haematemesis, haemodynamic compromise).
- **Resuscitate:** IV access ×2 wide-bore, cross-match 4 units, start crystalloid (e.g., 500 mL bolus if hypotensive). Target Hb >70 g/L (or >90 if significant comorbidities).
- **PPI bolus:** Pantoprazole 80 mg IV stat, then 8 mg/h infusion for 72 hours. Reduces rebleeding risk by suppressing acid and stabilising clot.
- **Call GI/Gastroenterology:** Urgent endoscopy within 24 hours for high-risk patients (Rockall ≥4). Use Glasgow-Blatchford score to determine urgency.

**Do NOT:** Give prokinetics (metoclopramide) – delays endoscopy. Avoid nasogastric tube unless needed for lavage (controversial, may dislodge clot).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Continue PPI infusion** (esomeprazole 8 mg/h) for 72 hours after successful endoscopic haemostasis (clip, thermal, or injection).
- **H. pylori treatment:** If positive, start triple therapy (e.g., Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID + Amoxicillin 1 g PO BID + Clarithromycin 500 mg PO BID × 14 days). For penicillin allergy: metronidazole 400 mg TID + clarithromycin + PPI. Quadruple therapy (bismuth-based) for high resistance areas.
- **Discontinue NSAIDs/aspirin:** If aspirin for secondary prevention, discuss risk–benefit with cardiology; often restart after haemostasis.
- **Monitor for rebleeding:** Tachycardia, drop in Hb, fresh haematemesis, melena. Repeat endoscopy if signs persist.
- **Diet:** Clear liquids after endoscopy; no restriction unless obstructed.

**Long-term:**
- **Healed ulcers:** Continue PPI (e.g., Omeprazole 20 mg once daily) for 4–8 weeks for duodenal, 8–12 weeks for gastric. Confirm healing with repeat endoscopy for gastric ulcers (biopsy scar).
- **H. pylori eradication confirmed** with urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after finishing antibiotics (off PPI for 2 weeks to avoid false negative).
- **If NSAID-related:** Switch to COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) + PPI if NSAID must continue. Consider misoprostol (200 µg QID) but side effects (diarrhoea, cramps) limit use.
- **Refractory ulcers:** Consider Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL; if borderline, test with secretin stimulation). Treat with high-dose PPI ± surgical excision of gastrinoma.
- **Prophylaxis in ICU/at-risk:** PPI or H₂RA (e.g., pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily) for stress ulcer prophylaxis in ventilated, coagulopathic, or post-surgical patients.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- Not biopsying a gastric ulcer (5–10% are malignant).
- Stopping antibiotics too early (must complete 14-day course).
- Resuming NSAIDs without gastroprotection.
- Ignoring positive H. pylori test result (must treat).
- Not checking for H. pylori iatrogenic false-negative (if patient on PPIs or recent antibiotics).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Peptic Ulcer Disease | Gastro-oesophageal Reflux (GERD) | Acute Pancreatitis | Biliary Colic / Cholecystitis |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Epigastric, burning, nocturnal, relieved by food (duodenal) or worsened (gastric) | Retrosternal burning, worsens when lying down, after meals | Sudden severe epigastric pain, radiates to back, relieved by leaning forward | RUQ/epigastric pain, crescendo, after fatty meal, radiates to right shoulder/scapula |
| **Exam** | Mild epigastric tenderness; if perforation → board-like rigidity, rebound | Normal or mild epigastric tenderness | Epigastric tenderness, guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen sign (rare) | Murphy’s sign (inspiratory arrest on palpation), RUQ tenderness |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless co-existing cardiac) | Normal | May have ST depression from ischaemia (rare) | Normal |
| **Labs** | Anaemia (if bleeding), positive H. pylori test | Normal | Elevated amylase/lipase (≥3× ULN), LFTs may be raised | Elevated ALP, GGT, bilirubin (if obstruction); normal amylase |
| **Key diff** | Endoscopy shows ulcer | Endoscopy shows oesophagitis or normal; 24h pH monitoring if uncertain | Imaging (CT) shows inflamed pancreas, necrosis | RUQ ultrasound shows gallstones, thick-walled gallbladder |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Upper GI bleeding** | Haematemesis (coffee-ground or red), melena, haematochezia (if massive), symptomatic anaemia, shock | Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, postural drop; Hb drop | Resuscitate, IV PPI, urgent endoscopy; Rockall score ≥4 → high risk; consider interventional radiology or surgery if endoscopy fails |
| **Perforation** | Sudden severe epigastric pain, generalised peritonitis, board-like rigidity | Erect CXR shows free air under diaphragm (75% sensitivity); CT abdomen with oral contrast is gold standard | Emergency laparotomy (open or laparoscopic) with omental patch (Graham patch) for duodenal repair; IV antibiotics, fluids, ICU |
| **Gastric outlet obstruction** | Early satiety, nausea/vomiting, weight loss, epigastric distension | Successive vomiting, visible peristalsis, succussion splash | NG decompression, correct electrolytes (hypokalaemic hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis); endoscopy to balloon dilate or stent; if refractory, surgical bypass (gastrojejunostomy) |
| **Penetration** | Pain shifts to back, becomes constant, no peritonitis | Back tenderness, raised amylase (mild due to inflammation of pancreas) | CT abdomen; high-dose PPI, surgery rarely needed unless fistula develops |
| **Malignant transformation** | Gastric ulcer that does not heal after 8 weeks of PPI; weight loss, anorexia | Endoscopic biopsy shows adenocarcinoma | Staging CT, surgical resection, oncology referral |

**Late-presentation stems:** “50-year-old with epigastric pain for 3 months, now vomiting undigested food, dehydrated, hypochloraemic alkalosis” = gastric outlet obstruction. “Patient with known duodenal ulcer presents with sudden severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, no bowel sounds” = perforation.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **H. pylori causes:** NSAIDs, Stress, H. pylori, Acid (hypersecretion), Alcohol, Smoking, Zollinger-Ellison → **“NSHAAZ”** (pronounced “n-shahz”)
- **Gastric vs Duodenal Ulcer:**  
  *Gastric: “G = Grumbling pain worse after Gulp”* (pain 30 min–1 hour after meals)  
  *Duodenal: “D = Delayed, Dusk, Dull”* (pain 2–3 hours after meals, nocturnal, relieved by food)
- **Complications of PUD:** Bleeding, Perforation, Obstruction, Penetration → **“BPOP”** (like “pop” the ulcer)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **No pain does not mean no ulcer** – especially in elderly on NSAIDs; look for insidious anaemia.
2. **Gastric ulcers must be biopsied (6–8 quadrants); duodenal ulcers need not be** – unless atypical.
3. **H. pylori eradication cures >90% of duodenal ulcers** – recurrence <10% if treatment is successful.
4. **PPI therapy should continue for 72 hours after endoscopic haemostasis** – decreases rebleeding from 15% to 5%.
5. **If a gastric ulcer does not heal after 8 weeks of high-dose PPI, re-endoscope and biopsy** – rule out malignancy.
6. **Stress ulcer prophylaxis is indicated only in ICU patients with coagulopathy, mechanical ventilation >48h, or history of PUD** – not for all hospitalised patients.
7. **Bleeding ulcer with a visible vessel = high risk** (rebleeding >50% without endoscopic therapy).

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking epigastric pain for cardiac ischaemia – always get an ECG.
- Starting H. pylori treatment before confirming the patient is not on PPIs or antibiotics (causes false negatives).
- Forgetting to check for iron deficiency anaemia in chronic melena – ferritin may be low.
- Not asking about NSAID/aspirin use – patients often don’t volunteer it.
- Assuming all epigastric pain is PUD – think pancreatitis, cholecystitis, cardiac.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **Haemodynamic instability:** Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1 L crystalloid, or heart rate >120 bpm (pulse >120 is ≥1000 mL blood loss).
- **Active red haematemesis** (massive bleed) or recurrent haematemesis after initial resuscitation.
- **Perforation:** Any sign of peritonitis (rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds) – call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Gastric outlet obstruction with severe electrolyte disturbance:** Potassium <2.5 mmol/L, pH >7.5 (metabolic alkalosis), or cardiac arrhythmia.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single parameter scoring 3, or aggregate 5+).

**Specific thresholds for critical care involvement:**
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (not correcting after initial fluid challenge).
- **Hb drop to <70 g/L** (or <80 g/L if coronary disease) despite blood transfusion – ongoing bleeding.
- **GCS ≤13** or any new confusion (hypovolaemic shock affecting cerebral perfusion).
- **Need for high-dependency unit:** planned endoscopic intervention with high-risk stigmata (e.g., adherent clot, visible vessel) – likely requires ICU/HDU post-procedure.

**When to call the crash team:** Cardiac arrest from massive haemorrhage (unlikely but possible) – activate local cardiac arrest protocol (CPR, massive transfusion, reverse anticoagulation).
